text: Greetings from BP Koirala Memorial Cancer Hospital, Varathpur, Nepal. Today we will be presenting a seventy-two years female with a history of dysphagia since last three months. Aphasia endoscopy showed a proliferative mass located at the distal one-third of the esophagus. Her CT scan and PET scan showed a localized disease at the distal one-third of the esophagus without any evidence of distant metastasis or the regional lymph node involvement.

We considered the patient for minimally invasive esophagectomy that consisted of three phases. First was the right vats esophagectomy with the lymph node clearance followed by the laparoscopic mobilization of the stomach and thirdly was the gastric block to the left side of the neck and making the gastroesophageal anastomosis today we'll be showing only the vast part we kept the patient under semi-form position with a single lumen endotracheal conventional tube we made the right lung collapsed using the co-to pneumothorax with the pressure of eight to nine mm of mercury with a flow rate of two to three liter per minute so today we'll be presenting with the vast part

The surgeon places three ports: a 10 mm optical port and two 5 mm working ports. The infrasigus dissection is performed, dividing the mediastinal pleura along the anterior border of the esophagus using a monopolar hook cautery. A gauze swab is placed inside the chest cavity for potential bleeding control and lung retraction. The semi-prone position aids visualization as the lung falls anteriorly, preventing obstruction of the operative field. The dissection proceeds from the level of the azygos arch downwards towards the diaphragm, with small vessels coagulated and divided using the hook cautery.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes lymphatic and fibrofatty tissue located at the right side of the right main bronchus, clearing the right main bronchi lymph node. The mediastinal pleura is divided along the posterior aspect of the esophagus, with caution taken to avoid injury to the azygos vein and aorta, which are visible adjacent to the esophagus. The esophagus is gently pushed towards the pulmonary hilum, creating a natural space between the aorta and the esophagus.

The surgeon divides direct branches of the artery from the aorta to the esophagus using bipolar cautery or a hook for smaller vessels. Fiber fatty tissue and lymphatics are cleared with the specimen. The mediastinal pleura along the anterior aspect of the esophagus is divided, with care taken near the trachea. The surgeon moves up to the apex of the chest, dividing flimsy adhesions between the lung and parietal pleura. The right subclavian artery is visible. The pulmonary pleura along the posterior aspect of the esophagus is gently dissected, pulling the esophagus towards the pulmonary hilum. Loose areolar tissue is visible, with caution advised around the aortic arch, trachea, and thoracic duct. The contralateral pleura is transparent, allowing visualization of the contralateral lung, which appears blackish and brownish.

The surgeon carefully separates the esophagus and lymphatic tissue from the membranous trachea, ensuring not to injure the trachea or contralateral pleura. Using traction and counter-traction techniques, the esophagus is retracted while the trachea remains fixed. The azygos arch is divided to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the right bronchial artery beneath the esophagus and assesses its caliber for division or preservation. The right vagus nerve is visible beneath the azygos arch, which is gradually mobilized by dividing the pleura at its superior and inferior aspects. The azygos arch is ligated using silk thread, first away from the superior vena cava and then towards it, allowing for its division with scissors. This provides clear exposure of the trachea, bronchi, and esophagus, facilitating further mobilization of the esophagus.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes lymph nodes located along the left main bronchus, identified as level L according to the Japanese classification. The left main bronchus is clearly visible during the procedure. The esophagus is gradually mobilized from surrounding structures, including the tracheobronchial lymph nodes. The right vagus nerve is visible, with efforts made to preserve its bronchial branches while cutting the esophageal branch.

The surgeon clears lymph nodes located at the subcarinal region, known as subcarinal lymph nodes or level 107, while preserving the bronchial branches and dividing the esophageal branch of the right vagus nerve. Care is taken to manage potential oozing from the lymph nodes using a vessel-sealing device such as a harmonic scalpel or advanced bipolar device.

The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to separate lymph nodes from the subcarinal region and the pericardium, clearly visualizing the trachea, right main bronchus, and left main bronchus. Smaller vessels from the subcarina to the lymph nodes are cauterized to prevent oozing.

The surgeon carefully separates the esophagus from flimsy adhesions connected to the tracheobronchial tree and the azygos vein. The aortic arch is visible in the surgical field. The esophagus is being meticulously separated from the membranous part of the trachea, with caution taken to avoid the nearby left recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon continues mobilizing the esophagus downward, revealing the lymph nodes located along the left main bronchus, known as level L. The pericardium is visible below, with the heart beating in front. The left vagal branches have been divided, and the tumor with associated lymph nodes is visible alongside the trachea and left main bronchus.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the esophagus up to the apex of the right chest, preserving the right recurrent laryngeal nerve near the right subclavian artery. The right subclavian artery is visible, and traction is applied to open tissue planes. The esophagus is then mobilized downwards, with fiber fatty tissue and lymphatics being removed en bloc with the specimen. The dissection continues until both diaphragmatic crura are visualized, with the inferior vena cava clearly visible.

The surgeon performs a minimally invasive esophagectomy in the prone position, ensuring clear visualization of the left main bronchus, trachea, and right main bronchus. The branches from the right vagus nerve to the right main bronchus are preserved. The procedure adheres to the principles of mesoesophagus, minimizing bleeding in the chest cavity. Remaining lymph nodes are removed at the end of the surgery.
